metadata.title: Why More Context Can Make the Output Worse

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. Keiko's 'nothing blue' constraint got one-fifteenth the weight of her brand guide purely because it was short and informal, and the campaign came back blue. I paste multi-document sets like hers constantly. Help me stop length from deciding authority: (1) label each of my sources as authoritative, current, or background so Claude stops weighting by page count; (2) prune the set down to only what changes the output; (3) surface any hard constraint buried in a short FYI doc so it can't get diluted.

props.command: Keiko's 'nothing blue' constraint got one-fifteenth the weight of her brand guide purely because it was short and informal, and the campaign came back blue. I paste multi-document sets like hers constantly. Help me stop length from deciding authority: (1) label each of my sources as authoritative, current, or background so Claude stops weighting by page count; (2) prune the set down to only what changes the output; (3) surface any hard constraint buried in a short FYI doc so it can't get diluted.
props.segment: Why More Context Can Make the

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. She pasted six documents — all about the same project. Claude produced a summary that reflected the longest, most formal one. The actual decision was in the short email at the bottom of the paste. It got almost no weight.

card.copy: Why more context can make the output worse
card.sub: unlabeled documents create false priority orders

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. Keiko is a marketing manager. She pastes a 15-page brand guide, a 6-page competitor analysis, and a one-page client brief marked only FYI. The brief contains a hard constraint: nothing blue. She asks Claude to write the campaign summary.
beats.scene_description: Marketing professional at a desk with multiple printed documents spread out, looking at screen, editorial newsprint treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, marketing manager at a desk with multiple stacked documents spread out, focused at a laptop screen, editorial documentary newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print collage

beats.narration_text: The output follows the brand guide. It is predominantly blue. The hard constraint — nothing blue — was in the unlabeled brief at the bottom of the paste. Why did more context produce worse output?

card.copy: Why did more context produce worse output?

beats.narration_text: A musician who loses the thread of their release campaign every time they open a new chat window is rebuilding from scratch — every single conversation. Persistent context means your promotion strategy accumulates instead of evaporating. The session that remembers what you said last week is the session that actually helps you run next week.

artifactLines: The same tools the platforms use — pointed back at you.
Roll your own. Own the deck.

beats.narration_text: Without source labels, Claude treats all pasted documents as a single undifferentiated block. It cannot determine which source is authoritative or current. So it weights them by what it can see: length, formality, structural completeness.

The brand guide is 15 pages — long and formally structured. The competitor analysis is 6 pages. The brief is 1 page, labeled FYI, informal. Length and formality strongly predict attention weight when no label assigns it explicitly.

The brief's constraint — nothing blue — gets proportional weight: roughly one fifteenth of the brand guide's influence. Not because it is less important. Because it is shorter and less formal. Unlabeled context creates a priority order you never chose.
Adding more unlabeled documents makes this worse, not better. Each new document adds noise and potentially buries the information that matters most. More is not more here — it is dilution.

card.copy: More unlabeled context = more false priority

beats.narration_text: The fix is label and prune. Label each source before it appears — tell Claude what it is and how to treat it. Then prune to what the task actually draws on. The brief becomes: Source A — primary, use this as the binding constraint. The rest are background.
With labels, Keiko assigns the priority order she actually intends. Claude treats the brief as binding — nothing blue is now a hard constraint, not a footnote in an ocean of blue brand assets.

This applies to any multi-document paste. Meeting notes plus a policy document plus an email thread: without labels, the formal policy dominates. With labels that say 'the email overrides the policy on the timeline,' the output reflects what you actually intended.

A school administrator pastes a 20-page district handbook, a 3-page teacher memo, and a 2-sentence email from the principal saying the decision in the memo is overridden. She asks Claude to summarize the current policy. Claude summarizes the handbook. The override email is invisible in the noise.

She adds two labels: handbook — background. Principal email — binding override on section 4. Now Claude weights the email appropriately. The summary reflects the override. Three words of labeling reversed the priority order.

Before pasting multiple documents: label each one. Use three categories: primary — use this as the binding source; background — for context, do not quote directly; override — this takes priority over all conflicting information above. Then prune each to what the task actually needs.
More unlabeled context gives Claude more to weight incorrectly. Label your sources — and the priority order you assign is the one Claude uses.

card.copy: Unlabeled context creates false priority orders.
card.sub: Label your sources. Assign the weight yourself.

With labels that say 'the email overrides the policy on the timeline,' the output reflects what you actually intended. A school administrator pastes a 20-page district handbook, a 3-page teacher memo, and a 2-sentence email from the principal saying the decision in the memo is overridden. She asks Claude to summarize the current policy. Claude summarizes the handbook. The override email is invisible in the noise.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
